The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.
== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|qq=|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|qq=|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11591047]] Wikidata name: shinme [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市 冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]] '''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals. [[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]
== Overview == [[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|qq=|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]] [[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ([[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]])]] Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.
Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''[[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]]''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.
The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591047]] Wikidata name: shinme [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市 冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]] '''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals. [[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]
== Overview == [[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]] [[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ([[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]])]] Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.
Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''[[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]]''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.
The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.
== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591047]] Wikidata name: shinme [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市 冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]] '''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals. [[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]
== Overview == [[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]] [[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ([[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]])]] Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.
Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''[[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]]''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.
The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.
== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Song Related to Shinme == * From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem: ** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」
== Shinme Gallery == <gallery> File:Shinme(Geku)02.jpg|Shinme in ceremonial attire (Toyōke Daijingū, Ise Jingu). File:Shinme(Naiku) 01.JPG|Resting Shinme at Ise Jingu Inner Shrine's stable. File:Sinme at isejingu.jpg|"Sakurasawa," Anglo-Arab Shinme from Imperial ranch (Toyōke Daijingū, Ise Jingu). File:Niukawakami-shimosha 201905 Shinme.jpg|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha, Nara. File:吉川八幡神社 鳥居と御神馬.jpg|"Izume," born July 23, 2017, Japanese half-blood, buckskin gelding symbolizing good harvest. File:小室浅間神社（下宮）神馬舎.jpg|Sacred stable at Komuro Sengen Shrine. </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591047]] Wikidata name: shinme [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市 冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]] '''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals. [[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]
== Overview == [[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]] [[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ([[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]])]] Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.
Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''[[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]]''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.
The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.
== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Song Related to Shinme == * From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem: ** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」
== Shinme Gallery == <gallery> File:Shinme(Geku)02.jpg|Shinme in ceremonial attire (Toyōke Daijingū, Ise Jingu). File:Shinme(Naiku) 01.JPG|Resting Shinme at Ise Jingu Inner Shrine's stable. File:Sinme at isejingu.jpg|"Sakurasawa," Anglo-Arab Shinme from Imperial ranch (Toyōke Daijingū, Ise Jingu). File:Niukawakami-shimosha 201905 Shinme.jpg|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha, Nara. File:吉川八幡神社 鳥居と御神馬.jpg|"Izume," born July 23, 2017, Japanese half-blood, buckskin gelding symbolizing good harvest. File:小室浅間神社（下宮）神馬舎.jpg|Sacred stable at Komuro Sengen Shrine. </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591047]] Wikidata name: shinme [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市 冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]] '''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals. [[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]
== Overview == [[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]] [[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ([[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]])]] Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.
Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''[[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]]''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.
The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.
== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Song Related to Shinme == * From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem: ** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」

File:Sinme at isejingu.jpg|"Sakurasawa," Anglo-Arab Shinme from Imperial ranch (Toyōke Daijingū, Ise Jingu). File:Niukawakami-shimosha 201905 Shinme.jpg|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha, Nara. File:吉川八幡神社 鳥居と御神馬.jpg|"Izume," born July 23, 2017, Japanese half-blood, buckskin gelding symbolizing good harvest. File:小室浅間神社（下宮）神馬舎.jpg|Sacred stable at Komuro Sengen Shrine. </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591047]] Wikidata name: shinme [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市 冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]] '''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals. [[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]
== Overview == [[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]] [[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ([[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]])]] Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.
Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''[[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]]''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.
The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.
== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Song Related to Shinme == * From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem: ** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」
== Shinme Gallery == <gallery> File:Shinme(Geku)02.jpg|Shinme in ceremonial attire (Toyōke Daijingū, Ise Jingu). File:Shinme(Naiku) 01.JPG|Resting Shinme at Ise Jingu Inner Shrine's stable. File:Sinme at isejingu.jpg|"Sakurasawa," Anglo-Arab Shinme from Imperial ranch (Toyōke Daijingū, Ise Jingu). File:Niukawakami-shimosha 201905 Shinme.jpg|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha, Nara. File:吉川八幡神社 鳥居と御神馬.jpg|"Izume," born July 23, 2017, Japanese half-blood, buckskin gelding symbolizing good harvest. File:小室浅間神社（下宮）神馬舎.jpg|Sacred stable at Komuro Sengen Shrine. </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Shinme}} * [[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]] * [[:en:Equine coat color|Horse coat colors]] - significance of horse colors in East Asia * [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] - known for horse dedication in rain prayers

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591047]] Wikidata name: shinme [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市 冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]] '''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals. [[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]
== Overview == [[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]] [[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ([[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]])]] Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.
Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''[[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]]''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.
The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.
== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Song Related to Shinme == * From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem: ** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591047]] Wikidata name: shinme [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市 冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]] '''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals. [[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]
== Overview == [[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]] [[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ([[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]])]] Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.
Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''[[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]]''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.
The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.
== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Song Related to Shinme == * From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem: ** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」
== Shinme Gallery == <gallery> File:Shinme(Geku)02.jpg|Shinme in ceremonial attire (Toyōke Daijingū, Ise Jingu). File:Shinme(Naiku) 01.JPG|Resting Shinme at Ise Jingu Inner Shrine's stable. File:Sinme at isejingu.jpg|"Sakurasawa," Anglo-Arab Shinme from Imperial ranch (Toyōke Daijingū, Ise Jingu). File:Niukawakami-shimosha 201905 Shinme.jpg|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha, Nara. File:吉川八幡神社 鳥居と御神馬.jpg|"Izume," born July 23, 2017, Japanese half-blood, buckskin gelding symbolizing good harvest. File:小室浅間神社（下宮）神馬舎.jpg|Sacred stable at Komuro Sengen Shrine. </gallery>

== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Song Related to Shinme == * From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem: ** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591047]] Wikidata name: shinme [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市 冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]] '''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals. [[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]
== Overview == [[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]] [[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ([[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]])]] Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.
Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''[[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]]''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.
The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.
== Shrines Keeping Shinme == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]] * [[:en:Kanda Shrine|Kanda Shrine]] * {{ill|Hiratsuka Hachimangu|ja|平塚八幡宮|1=Hiratsuka Hachimangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiratsuka Hachimangu|qq=}} * [[:en:Tado Taisha|Tado Taisha]] * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingu]] * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]] * {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]] * {{ill|Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|ja|吉川八幡神社 (豊能町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Hachiman Shrine (Toyono)|1=Yoshikawa-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17230661}} * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|lt=Soma Nakamura Shrine|ja|相馬中村神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soma Nakamura Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gu]] * {{ill|Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine|1=Fujisan Shimomiya Omuro Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459659}} * {{ill|Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|ja|石切剣箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja|大和国 鹿島香取本宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Katori Hongu (Yamato Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|ja|丹生川上神社下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Niukawakami Shrine Shimosha|WD=Q10877367}} * {{ill|Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja|大石神社 (京都市山科区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oishi Shrine (Kyoto Yamashina)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Song Related to Shinme == * From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem: ** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」

According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinme]][[en:Shinme]][[ja:神馬]][[zh:神馬]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591047]] Wikidata name: shinme [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市 冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]] '''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals. [[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]
== Overview == [[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]] [[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ([[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]])]] Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.
Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''[[:en:Ema (Shinto)|Ema (Shinto)]]''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.
The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to [[:en:Niukawakami Shrine|Niukawakami Shrine]] in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.
Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.
According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.

'''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals.

== Overview ==
[[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]]
[[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Hotaka Shrine|en|Hotaka Shrine|WD=Q11597109|lt=Hotaka Shrine}})]]
Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.

Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''{{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|WD=Q687288|lt=Ema (Shinto)}}''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.

The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine|en|Niukawakami Shrine|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine}} in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.

Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.

According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.

Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.

According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.

[[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市　冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]]
'''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals.
[[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingu}}). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]

== Overview ==
[[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]]
[[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Hotaka Shrine|en|Hotaka Shrine|WD=Q11597109|lt=Hotaka Shrine}})]]
Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.

Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''{{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|WD=Q687288|lt=Ema (Shinto)}}''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.

The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine|en|Niukawakami Shrine|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine}} in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.

Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.

According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.

== Shrines Keeping Shinme ==

[[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]]

Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.

Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''{{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|WD=Q687288|lt=Ema (Shinto)}}''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.

The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine|en|Niukawakami Shrine|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine}} in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.

Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.

According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.

== Shrines Keeping Shinme ==

== Song Related to Shinme ==
* From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem:
** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」

[[File:冨士山下宮小室浅間神社_JRA奉納_神馬_瑞桜（ヒカリシャーク）.jpg|alt=奉納された神馬。競走馬を引退したサラブレッドが、神馬として奉納されている（山梨県富士吉田市　冨士山下宮小室浅間神社）|thumb]]
'''Shinme''' (神馬, also read as Jinme or Kami-uma) refers to sacred horses revered as mounts for the gods in Japanese Shinto shrines. These horses, typically white, are dedicated to shrines or appear during festivals.
[[Image:Shinme(Geku) 01.JPG|thumb|240px|alt=Shinme(Geku)_01.JPG|Sacred stable at {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingu|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}). Shinme named "Okaaki."]]

== Overview ==
[[File:Shinme - KagawaKen-Gokoku-jinja.jpg|thumb|200px|Metal statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|香川縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|1=Kagawa Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667575}})]]
[[File:Hotaka-jinja14bs5s2040.jpg|thumb|200px|Wooden statue of a Shinme ({{ill|Hotaka Shrine|en|Hotaka Shrine|WD=Q11597109|lt=Hotaka Shrine|1=Hotaka Shrine}})]]
Since the Nara period, it has been customary to dedicate horses to shrines as prayers. Donors range from commoners to imperial family members.

Due to the high cost and maintenance burden, many shrines replaced real horses with '''{{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|WD=Q687288|lt=Ema (Shinto)|1=Ema (Shinto)}}''' (horse paintings)<ref>For instance, historical texts such as the "Shoku Nihongi" and "Engishiki" mention dedicating black horses for rain, later replaced by Ema in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}.</ref>. Life-sized horse statues also commonly serve as Shinme.

The "Engishiki" (Vol. 3, Clause 26) states black horses were dedicated for rain prayers<ref>Example: "Shoku Nihongi," Hōki 3 (772), February 24th, describes dedicating a black horse to {{ill|Niukawakami Shrine|en|Niukawakami Shrine|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}} in Yamato Province during droughts.</ref>, and white horses for clear weather. This tradition is echoed in the Noh play {{ill|Ema (Noh)|ja|絵馬 (能)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema (Noh)|qq=}}<ref>Gondō Yoshikazu, "Nōgaku Techō," Shinjindō, 1979, pp.58-59.</ref>. Medieval warriors also dedicated horses for victory prayers.

Shrines often maintain "Shinme-sha" or "Shinkyu-sha" (sacred stables), even without actual horses, reflecting this tradition. Retired thoroughbred racehorses are sometimes dedicated as Shinme.

According to the Chinese text "Furui Zu," a blue-black horse with white mane and tail was considered auspicious. The "Shoku Nihongi" also records a sacred horse from Higo Province presented in 768.

== Song Related to Shinme ==
* From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem:
** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」

== Song Related to Shinme ==
* From the "Azuma Kagami," Kenkyū 6 (1195), April 27, Kajiwara Kagetoki dedicated a Shinme at Sumiyoshi Shrine with this poem:
** 「我が君の 手向の駒を 引きつれて 行末遠き しるしあらわせ」
